headline: The Skeeters Are Back, North Correa Keeps Breaking Records They Don't Deserve, and the Benchwarmers Are Living on Borrowed Time
subheadline: Week 7 delivered a bounce-back performance for the ages, a stolen base record that somehow ended in defeat, and a thriller so close it probably needs a recount.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Buttville Skidmarks, 4-3

Look, I've watched a lot of close fantasy matchups in my life (too many, honestly — this is a cry for help), and the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** beating the **Buttville Skidmarks** 4-3 is the kind of result that makes you stare at your phone at midnight wondering how that's even possible. The Skidmarks threw up a 3.53 ERA, pitched 71.1 innings — second most in the league this week — and posted 8 NSVH, which tied for the league lead. They had more innings, a better ERA on paper, and a massive NSVH advantage. By conventional logic, they should have won this thing going away.

And yet. Davis Martin went out there and posted 19 strikeouts with a team ERA of 1.71 — the new season record for lowest ERA by a winning team, by the way — and the Reapers somehow clawed out enough categories to steal this thing. It's the fantasy equivalent of getting outgained by 150 yards and still winning 13-10 on three field goals and a fumble return. (Bills fans know this energy intimately. It's not comfortable.) The Reapers didn't dominate. They just... won the categories that mattered.

For the Skidmarks, this loss is the kind that leaves a mark on your soul. (No pun intended, but I'm keeping it.) They have real pitching talent — this is genuinely a good staff — but if you can't score runs, you can't win close matchups, and close matchups are where they keep finding themselves. This team needs a serious offensive injection, and they needed it about three weeks ago.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** came into Week 7 with something to prove. After getting embarrassed in Week 6 — one of those blowout losses that makes you wonder if the preseason hype was all hot air — they responded with what might be the most complete fantasy week anyone has put together all season. A 9-1 demolition of the **Canberra Wombats** with 12 HR, 36 RBI, 35 runs, a 2.08 ERA, 6 quality starts, and 9 NSVH. That's not a good week. That's a "we are genuinely different from everyone else in this league" week.

The pitching story is Eduardo Rodriguez, who was basically unhittable — a 0.59 ERA over 15.1 innings, which in fantasy baseball terms is like watching a video game player on easy mode. The offense was firing from every angle: league-leading home runs, league-leading RBI, league-leading runs scored. The Skeeters led the league in IP at 73.2 innings, posted the second-lowest ERA in the league, and had the second-lowest WHIP at 0.91. When you are simultaneously among the best in the league in both pitching and hitting categories in the same week, that's not luck — that's a roster operating at full capacity.

What makes this performance matter beyond just the W is the timing. The Skeeters had the "wait, are they actually good?" cloud hanging over them after last week's collapse. This week they didn't just answer the question — they answered it with a megaphone. And here's the part that should terrify the rest of the league: their Week 8 matchup is against **General Hospital**, who just got exposed by **BaseballPro77** and is now in genuine must-win territory. The Skeeters are about to feast again, and there's very little reason to think they won't.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Albuquerque Bombers tie Athletic Park Dragons, 5-5

The **Albuquerque Bombers** posted a 5.15 ERA and a 1.47 WHIP this week. Under normal circumstances, that's a losing ticket. But Byron Buxton's 3 HR, Trevor Naylor's 3 SB, and the broader offensive output — 33 runs, 11 HR, and a .348 OBP — kept them in enough categories to escape with a tie. The **Athletic Park Dragons**, meanwhile, had Cristopher Sánchez's 0.67 WHIP and Aaron Judge's 3 HR working for them, plus 5 quality starts and 6 NSVH — genuinely good pitching marks. A tie for the Dragons after this performance is a mild disappointment. For the Bombers, facing the Hiroshima pitching staff next week with that rotation is not something I'd be circling on the calendar with a smiley face.

### Riverside Roughnecks def. Abbotsford-Colby Twins, 7-3

The **Riverside Roughnecks** did what good managers do — they saw an opportunity, attacked the waiver wire with conviction, and backed it up with a 7-3 win. Seiya Suzuki and Xavier Edwards provided the offensive punch, and the rotation, even before the new additions fully kick in, held together well enough. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** had the second-most stolen bases in the league (9 SB), which sounds great until you realize they posted zero NSVH and only 48 innings pitched — that's unsustainable. The Twins adding Chase Dollander is the right move, but this week was a tough one all around. The Roughnecks are quietly becoming a team I don't want to face in a must-win situation.

### BaseballPro77 def. General Hospital, 6-4

This was a measuring-stick game, and **BaseballPro77** passed the test. Bobby Witt Jr.'s 3 HR and 7 runs, Bryce Elder's 1.54 ERA, and Logan Gilbert's 0.75 WHIP powered a legitimate 6-4 win over a **General Hospital** squad that had real firepower — 10 HR, 32 RBI, and 6 NSVH are not nothing. Andy Pages had 4 HR and 8 RBI, Kyle Tucker's .478 OBP is the kind of number that makes people jealous at their work-from-home desk. But General Hospital's pitching (4.87 ERA, 1.31 WHIP) was the difference, and BaseballPro77 exploited it. The implication here is significant: General Hospital is a genuine threat offensively, but if the pitching doesn't firm up before the Week 8 Skeeters matchup, they're looking at back-to-back bad losses.

### Benchwarmers def. North Correa, 5-4

This one still doesn't feel right. **North Correa** posted 12 stolen bases (a league record, more on that in a minute), 31 runs, and a reasonable pitching line — and still lost 5-4. The **Benchwarmers** had Cody Bellinger's 8 RBI and Moises Dubón's 5 RBI doing the heavy lifting, along with 9 HR and 33 RBI — the second-most RBI in the league this week. But zero NSVH and only 2 quality starts means the Benchwarmers are basically surviving on offensive production while their pitching is a screen door on a submarine. North Correa, meanwhile, had 3 home runs all week. Three. In a week where they ran the bases like Rickey Henderson reincarnated, they also hit like a pitcher in a 2003 AL game. The imbalance is genuinely impressive in a frustrating way.

### Sugar Land Skeeters def. Canberra Wombats, 9-1

Already covered above, but the **Canberra Wombats** deserve their own moment of acknowledgment here — and not a good one. A .254 OBP. Fourteen runs scored. Dropping Jake Bennett. And Jacob deGrom posting a 4.05 ERA, which, if you've been betting on a deGrom resurgence, is not the week you wanted to see. The Wombats' offense is the worst in the league this week by a significant margin, and when you're getting 69 strikeouts from your pitching staff and still losing 9-1, the problem isn't the arms. It's everything else.

### Hiroshima Radiation def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 6-4

The **Hiroshima Radiation** have quietly put together the most balanced roster in the league. Bryce Harper's 4 HR and 7 runs, Kyle Schwarber's 5 HR, and a league-high 7 quality starts with a 2.58 ERA — that's the kind of week that makes contenders nervous. The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** actually pitched pretty well (3.45 ERA, 6 QS, league-leading 73 strikeouts from their staff), and Pete Alonso contributed 7 RBI. This team is not as bad as their record suggests. But they lost anyway, which is the story of their season. The luck delta is brutal for them, and frankly, it's starting to feel personal.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Luckiest: Benchwarmers.** The numbers don't lie and they don't care about your feelings. The Benchwarmers carry the largest positive luck delta in the entire league at +1.62, and this week's 5-4 win over North Correa was a perfect illustration of why. North Correa posted more stolen bases, more runs, and a better overall pitching line — and still lost. The Benchwarmers survived on RBI production and thin edges in power categories. There's no version of "sustainable" that applies to what this team is doing right now. They are the fantasy equivalent of a team going 8-0 in one-possession games — eventually the regression comes, and it is not going to be pretty.

**Unluckiest: Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs.** Seventy-three strikeouts. Six quality starts. A 3.45 ERA. Thirty-two RBI. These are legitimately competitive numbers across multiple categories, and the Wonderdogs still lost 6-4. Their season-long luck delta of -0.89 is the worst in the league, which means this isn't a weekly thing — it's a pattern. This team is consistently delivering more value than their record reflects, and if Tyler Mahle returns to form and the schedule ever evens out, they could make some noise. But right now they're the best bad-record team in the league, and that's a deeply uncomfortable place to exist.

---

## League Record Alert

North Correa tied the season record for most stolen bases in a single week with 12 SB — and here's the part that makes it both impressive and completely on-brand for this team: the previous record was also set by North Correa. So they have now set the record and tied their own record, all while losing the matchup 5-4. It's like breaking the world record for most points scored in a loss. Technically historic. Practically devastating. The record stands. The W does not.

---

## Manager Spotlight: General Hospital

Let's talk about **General Hospital**, because Week 8 is shaping up to be a defining moment for this franchise.

Right now they're sitting at 9th in the power rankings, which stings when you look at the raw talent on this roster. Andy Pages had a genuine breakout week — 4 HR, 8 RBI, the kind of performance that makes you feel great about your roster construction right up until you check the final score. Kyle Tucker's .478 OBP is borderline absurd and represents legitimate trade-value peak territory. This team has weapons. Real ones.

The problem is the pitching. A 4.87 ERA and a 1.31 WHIP against a BaseballPro77 squad that isn't even in the top three offensively is not the kind of showing that instills confidence. They had 6 NSVH — the category leader-adjacent mark — but if the starters can't hold leads, saves and holds are a band-aid on a broken leg. And now they get the Skeeters in Week 8, fresh off the most dominant performance of the season. General Hospital needs to find at least two arms who can give them a 3.50 ERA week or this could turn into a 10th-place situation faster than anyone expected. The window to make a statement is closing.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Andy Pages, General Hospital — SELL.** Four home runs and 8 RBI in a single week is as good as his trade value is going to look all season. If you can get a return that upgrades your pitching — which General Hospital desperately needs — this is the week to make that call. Waiting for another 4-HR week is the kind of gamble that ends with you at 7th place in December wondering what happened.

**Kyle Tucker, General Hospital — HOLD.** A .478 OBP isn't a fluke — it reflects real plate discipline and on-base ability week after week. Tucker is a genuine building block, not a "sell high" piece. Unless someone is offering you something genuinely transformational, you hold and let this play out.

**Davis Martin, Wisconsin Rapids Reapers — BUY.** Nineteen strikeouts. A 1.71 team ERA that set the season record. If you can acquire Martin before the rest of the league figures out what the Reapers' manager already knows, you're getting a steal. The underlying stuff is real. Do the work.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | A 9-1 demolition of Canberra with 12 HR, 36 RBI, 6 QS, and Eduardo Rodriguez at 0.59 ERA is the most complete week any team has had in weeks — the Skeeters are back and dangerous [UP]
2. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Bryce Harper's 4 HR and 7 runs, Schwarber's 5 HR, and a league-high 7 QS with a 2.58 ERA make this the most balanced roster in the league right now; the offense finally showed up alongside elite pitching [UP]
3. **BaseballPro77** | Bobby Witt Jr.'s 3 HR and 7 runs, Bryce Elder's 1.54 ERA, and Logan Gilbert's 0.75 WHIP powered a 6-4 win over General Hospital; this team just won a measuring-stick game and looks like a genuine contender [SAME]
4. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Davis Martin's 19 Ks and a team ERA of 1.71 — the new season record for lowest ERA by a winning team — are elite outputs; a 4-3 grind over Buttville with real pitching depth forming is a dangerous combination [UP]
5. **Athletic Park Dragons** | A 5-5 tie with the Bombers is a mild disappointment given Cristopher Sánchez's 0.67 WHIP and Aaron Judge's 3 HR, but the pitching staff remains the league's most improved and a QS total of 5 is a weekly anchor [DOWN]
6. **Riverside Roughnecks** | A convincing 7-3 win over the Twins backed by Seiya Suzuki, Xavier Edwards, and the massive waiver haul of Roki Sasaki, Bassitt, and Fedde signals real intent; this rotation could look completely different in two weeks [UP]
7. **Albuquerque Bombers** | A 5-5 tie when your pitching posts a 5.15 ERA and 1.47 WHIP is honestly a small miracle — Buxton's 3 HR, Naylor's 3 SB, and Turang's 2 HR kept it respectable, but the rotation is a serious concern heading into a Hiroshima matchup [DOWN]
8. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | The 3-7 loss hides a decent offensive week from Ramírez and Wilson, but dropping to zero NSVH and posting only 48 IP is unsustainable; the aggressive waiver wire work adding Dollander and Montero is the right call [DOWN]
9. **General Hospital** | Andy Pages' 4 HR and 8 RBI and Kyle Tucker's .478 OBP are trade-value peaks, but losing 6-4 despite 10 HR, 32 RBI, and 6 NSVH to BaseballPro77 stings — a Week 8 showdown with the Skeeters is now a must-win situation [DOWN]
10. **North Correa** | Posting a league-record-tying 12 SB, 31 runs, and 2 solid starts while still losing 5-4 is the definition of this team's season — elite speed and respectable pitching undercut by a total lack of power; 3 HR all week is unworkable [SAME]
11. **Benchwarmers** | Cody Bellinger's 8 RBI and Dubón's 5 RBI are nice, but this team's +1.62 luck delta is an unsustainable house of cards; zero NSVH and a rotation with only 2 QS means the underlying talent cannot support their record long-term [SAME]
12. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Pete Alonso's 7 RBI, 6 QS, and 73 Ks are legitimate — this team is not as bad as their record suggests, and the -0.89 luck delta is a real injustice; Tyler Mahle adds rotation depth but they need the schedule to even out [UP]
13. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Eight NSVH and a 3.53 ERA are genuinely good pitching marks but losing 4-3 to a Reapers team with fewer innings and lower power output is a familiar story — this roster cannot score enough runs to win close matchups [SAME]
14. **Canberra Wombats** | Getting blown out 9-1 with a .254 OBP and only 14 runs scored while dropping Jake Bennett off the roster is moving in the wrong direction on every front; deGrom's 4.05 ERA is concerning and the offense is the worst in the league this week [DOWN]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[A] Riverside Roughnecks** — Added Roki Sasaki, Chris Bassitt, Erick Fedde, Travis Bazzana; dropped four relievers and a utility piece — This is a transformational haul, full stop. Sasaki alone is a top-50 SP add if healthy, Bassitt is your reliable innings-eater who gives you 6 innings of 3.50 ball every five days like clockwork, and Fedde provides rotation depth so you're not scrambling when someone gets scratched. Pairing this with an already-improving pitching staff makes the Roughnecks a genuine sleeper threat over the next month. This is exactly the kind of aggressive, decisive waiver move that separates managers who win leagues from managers who finish fifth and complain about luck. (We all know that guy. Don't be that guy.)

**[B] Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — Added Daniel Lynch IV, Braydon Fisher, Kevin Kelly, Brent Headrick; dropped Drew Pomeranz — Loading up on bullpen arms after posting a 1.71 ERA week tells you everything about what this manager is trying to do: lock in NSVH and ERA as identity categories and grind opponents out. The quality of individual adds is modest — none of these names are making anyone's "stash of the week" column — but the volume and timing make sense if you're playing a long game on pitching ratios. Smart if not spectacular.

**[B] Hiroshima Radiation** — Added Trevor McDonald, George Soriano, Gus Varland, Kyle Karros; dropped Ryan Walker, Carlos Correa — The headline here is that McDonald immediately justified his add with a 1.29 ERA and a 0.29 WHIP in 7 innings on debut. That's the dream waiver wire scenario right there — you take the flier and the guy goes out and pitches like a Cy Young contender in week one. Dropping Carlos Correa is a bold call that signals a clear pivot away from name-value roster management toward active contributors, which is exactly the right philosophy at this stage of the season. Hiroshima keeps getting smarter.

**[B] Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — Added Chase Dollander, Keider Montero, Chase Meidroth; dropped Grant Holmes, Andrew Painter, Caleb Durbin — Dollander is the headliner and a legitimately exciting add — high-upside SP with front-of-rotation potential if everything clicks. The aggressive rebuild of the pitching staff after a rough week (zero NSVH, 48 IP) shows real urgency and self-awareness from the manager. Montero is a deeper stash play — you're not rostering him to win this week's ERA category, you're rostering him because you're playing the long game. Good instincts all around, even if the results take a few weeks to show up.

**[C] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Tyler Mahle, Matthew Liberatore, Ian Seymour; dropped Justin Bruihl, Cam Smith (and somehow re-added then dropped Liberatore in the same week) — The Mahle add is legitimate. If he's returning to form, he's a real rotation piece and the Wonderdogs need all the pitching depth they can get. But the Liberatore ping-pong situation is... concerning? Like watching someone open the refrigerator, close it, open it again, and then walk away empty-handed. The bigger issue is that this team needs bats more urgently than pitching depth — they led the league in strikeouts this week with 73 Ks and still lost 6-4. Adding Seymour when your offense posted a .247 OBP is solving the wrong problem. But hey, at least they're doing *something*.
